_note: Representative sample of public review-site feedback about SecurityScorecard, grounded in the themes that recur across Capterra / G2 / TrustRadius (rating volatility & attribution disputes, questionnaire/portfolio workflow friction, board-reporting value, remediation guidance, pricing). This is DEMO data used to seed the ingestion panel when a live scrape is blocked by bot walls; it is not a verbatim copy of any single review. runner/scrape-reviews.ts attempts the live scrape first and records which sources were reachable.

items.title: Board-ready risk narrative in minutes
items.body: The A-F letter grade is the single most useful thing for me. I can put one slide in front of the audit committee and everyone immediately understands where we stand versus peers. Drilling from the grade into the specific factors is intuitive, and the peer benchmarking helped me justify next year's budget.

items.title: Score volatility undermines the exec conversation
items.body: We remediated a finding, confirmed it externally, and it still took almost three weeks for the score to move. In the meantime the board asked why our rating dropped. The outside-in signal is valuable but the lag between fixing something and seeing it reflected makes it hard to trust in front of leadership.

items.title: Attribution false positives cost us real hours
items.body: Several IP addresses flagged against us simply are not ours — they belong to a former hosting provider. Disputing attribution is a slow ticket-based process and the findings keep resurfacing. When you are trying to hold vendors accountable to their own scores, having your own score be wrong is a credibility problem.

items.title: Best tool we have for monitoring 500+ vendors
items.body: Continuous monitoring across our whole third-party portfolio is exactly what we needed. The alerting when a critical vendor's grade drops lets my small team focus diligence where it actually matters instead of chasing everyone equally. Portfolio views are strong.

items.title: Questionnaire workflow is clunky
items.body: Sending and chasing security questionnaires through the Atlas workflow is more manual than I expected. Vendors get confused by the portal, responses stall, and mapping their answers back to our internal risk register is copy-paste. It works, but it does not save the time I hoped when we bought it for TPRM.

items.title: Remediation guidance is genuinely actionable
items.body: What sets it apart from other rating tools is that each finding comes with a clear description of what to fix and why it matters. My team can hand the remediation notes straight to IT without translating. That closed-loop guidance is why we renewed.

items.title: Pricing scales painfully as you add vendors
items.body: The platform is good but the per-vendor pricing model meant our renewal quote jumped well beyond budget once we wanted continuous monitoring on the full supplier base. We had to cut the number of monitored vendors, which defeats the purpose of portfolio-wide visibility.

items.title: Great for demonstrating posture to customers
items.body: As a CSM I use our own scorecard in QBRs to show customers we take security seriously, and I use their scorecards to move deals forward when procurement raises a security objection. Having a shared, third-party-validated number cuts through a lot of back-and-forth.

items.title: Powerful but a steep learning curve
items.body: There is a lot here and the initial onboarding was heavier than expected. Once you understand the factor weighting it is powerful, but new analysts on my team take a while to become productive. Better in-product guidance would help adoption.

items.title: Outside-in view we could not get elsewhere
items.body: Seeing our attack surface the way an outsider (or an attacker) would is invaluable. It surfaced an exposed service two of our internal scans had missed. For continuous external monitoring it earns its place in the stack.

items.title: Reporting is good but not flexible enough
items.body: The out-of-the-box board and regulator reports are a strong start, but I frequently need to reformat them for our specific audit committee template. Custom report building is limited, so I end up exporting to slides and rebuilding. More layout control would save me hours each quarter.

items.title: Vendor detail pages speed up diligence
items.body: When onboarding a new supplier I can pull their scorecard, see the historical trend, and decide in minutes whether we need a deeper questionnaire. It has genuinely shortened our vendor onboarding cycle time.

items.title: Disputing findings needs to be self-service
items.body: The evidence is usually right, but when it is wrong the correction path relies on support tickets and long turnaround. For a platform whose whole value is trust in the number, letting customers submit and track disputes in-product would be a big improvement.

items.title: Alerts keep our critical vendors honest
items.body: We set escalation thresholds on tier-1 vendors and the alerting has flagged real degradations twice this year, letting us open a conversation before it became an incident. That proactive signal is the core of why we keep it.

items.title: Score dropped with no clear explanation
items.body: Our grade fell a full letter and the factor breakdown did not obviously explain why. Support eventually traced it to a third-party CDN range, but the initial lack of a clear 'here is exactly what changed' timeline caused a fire drill internally. Change transparency needs work.

items.title: Solid platform for a lean security team
items.body: With a flat headcount, the prioritized findings let us punch above our weight. We fix what moves the needle first and leave the noise. The severity ranking is mostly sensible and saves us from boiling the ocean.

items.title: Useful in sales cycles, occasional data lag
items.body: Our solutions engineering team leans on prospect scorecards during evaluations. Mostly accurate, but once or twice the data was stale enough that a prospect pushed back and we lost momentum. When it is current it is a great door-opener.

items.title: Cut our cyber-insurance friction
items.body: We shared our scorecard trend with our insurer at renewal and it demonstrably helped the conversation about premium. Having an independent, third-party attestation of posture is worth a lot when everything else is self-reported.

items.title: Portfolio import could be smoother
items.body: Bulk-adding our vendor list meant a lot of manual matching to the right company entity, and a few subsidiaries mapped to the wrong parent. Once set up it is fine, but initial portfolio construction was more hands-on than the demo suggested.

items.title: The peer benchmarking is a budget-winner
items.body: Being able to say 'we are at the 70th percentile for our industry and here is the gap to the top decile' turned an abstract security ask into a concrete board decision. That comparative framing is the feature I would miss most.

items.title: Integrations are thinner than advertised
items.body: We wanted findings to flow into our GRC tool and ticketing automatically. The available connectors covered some of it but we ended up building against the API for the rest. Budget for engineering time if deep integration matters to you.

items.title: Renewal conversations are easier with the data
items.body: On the account team, I use adoption and score-improvement data to frame renewals. When a customer can see their posture improved on our watch, the renewal basically sells itself. I wish the in-app account-health view for us as a vendor was as rich as the security data.

items.title: Continuous monitoring replaced our annual scramble
items.body: We used to do point-in-time vendor assessments once a year. Now it is continuous and the exceptions come to us. For a TPRM function that is a completely different operating model and far less stressful at audit time.

items.title: Support response times slipped this year
items.body: Earlier on, support was excellent. Over the last two quarters ticket turnaround has grown and a couple of attribution disputes sat open far too long. The product is still good; the service experience is what is testing our patience at renewal.

items.title: Good coverage of our industry peers
items.body: The data depth for our sector is strong, so benchmarking feels credible rather than a rough proxy. Some smaller international vendors have thinner data, but for our core supplier base the coverage is exactly what we need for continuous oversight.

items.title: Does what it says, onboarding needs a human
items.body: The platform delivers on external ratings and monitoring. Just do not expect to self-serve the setup — you will want your CSM heavily involved for the first month to configure portfolios, alerts, and reports correctly. Once configured it hums.

items.title: The evidence trail satisfies our auditors
items.body: Every finding has an evidence artifact and a timestamp, which our external auditors accept without pushback. For regulated financial services that defensibility is worth more than the score itself some quarters.

items.title: Too many low-value findings dilute the signal
items.body: A chunk of the findings are informational noise that will never be prioritized, and they still count against the grade in ways customers question. Better separation of 'this materially affects risk' from 'this is cosmetic' would make the number more defensible.

items.title: Indispensable for our vendor risk program
items.body: Between continuous monitoring, questionnaire management, and the reporting, it is the backbone of our TPRM program now. The questionnaire piece still has rough edges but the portfolio-level risk visibility is best in class.

items.title: Mobile and quick-glance experience is limited
items.body: Most of my exec stakeholders want a quick 'are we green' glance on their phone between meetings. The mobile experience is basically the desktop site, so I end up screenshotting the grade into a message. A lightweight exec summary view would land well.

items.title: Sales enablement value is underrated
items.body: People buy this for security, but our GTM team gets a ton of value using scorecards to accelerate deals stuck in security review. When a prospect sees a strong, independent grade, the security objection largely goes away. Wish there were built-in shareable formats for that.

items.title: Trend history tells the real story
items.body: A single grade is a snapshot; the historical trend is what I actually present. Being able to show twelve months of steady improvement to the board turns security from a cost center narrative into a progress narrative.

items.title: Wish remediation status synced both ways
items.body: When we fix something, we still have to wait for the next scan and cannot easily tell the platform 'this is remediated, please reverify.' A way to signal remediation and trigger reassessment would shorten the loop that today feels like waiting in the dark.
